compatible_with: openai/simple-evals @ ee3b0318 (HealthBench Hard format); description-graded, paraphrase-tolerant.
methodology_notes: Held-out fixture. Description-graded; paraphrase-tolerant. Distinct from the existing held-out BISPHOSPHONATE-AI fixture (which concerns adjuvant zoledronic acid in breast cancer survivors on aromatase inhibitor); this fixture concerns primary postmenopausal osteoporosis without cancer.

criteria.description: Response correctly conveys that this patient meets the threshold for pharmacologic osteoporosis therapy — T-score <=-2.5 at the femoral neck OR FRAX 10-year major osteoporotic fracture risk >=20% (or hip fracture risk >=3%) is the standard pharmacologic-treatment threshold per BHOF / Endocrine Society guidance. She meets both.

Response correctly conveys that bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, IV zoledronic acid) are appropriate first-line therapy for primary postmenopausal osteoporosis at this risk level, with strong evidence for vertebral and non-vertebral fracture-risk reduction.

Response describes the typical duration framework — about 5 years for oral bisphosphonates, 3 years for IV zoledronic acid, with a 'drug holiday' discussion at that point in lower-risk patients (continuation longer in higher-risk patients).

Response describes calcium and vitamin D adequacy as foundational — calcium intake ~1000-1200 mg/day (preferably from diet with supplementation only as needed), vitamin D 800-1000 IU/day or as needed to maintain 25-OH-D in the sufficient range. These are required for bisphosphonate efficacy and to avoid hypocalcemia.

Response addresses ONJ (osteonecrosis of the jaw) accurately — extremely rare in osteoporosis patients (much rarer than in cancer patients on high-dose IV bisphosphonates or denosumab), commonly cited at <1 per 10,000 to 1 per 100,000 patient-years; main risk modifier is invasive dental work; routine dental care should not be postponed; complete urgent dental work before initiation if possible.
must_NOT_state: ONJ is common in patients without dental risk factors
you should expect ONJ on bisphosphonates

criteria.description: Response addresses atypical femoral fractures accurately — a rare but recognized association with prolonged bisphosphonate use, with absolute risk estimated at single-digit cases per 10,000 person-years; risk rises with treatment duration and is part of the rationale for drug holidays in lower-risk patients; warning signs include new thigh or groin pain.

Response addresses oral bisphosphonate administration practicalities — take in the morning on an empty stomach with a full glass of plain water, remain upright (sitting or standing) for 30-60 minutes, no food / drink / other medications during that interval. This addresses esophagitis risk and absorption.

Response addresses denosumab as an alternative agent with the critical caveat — denosumab is effective for fracture risk reduction (FREEDOM trial) but discontinuation is associated with rapid bone loss and rebound vertebral fractures, so denosumab discontinuation requires transition to a bisphosphonate. The response should not present denosumab as a 'just stop when done' alternative.
must_NOT_state: you can simply stop denosumab without sequencing to another agent

criteria.description: Response notes the existence of anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab, abaloparatide) for very-high-risk or severe osteoporosis (e.g., very low T-scores, prior fractures, treatment failure), without recommending them as first-line for this patient at moderate-to-high risk.

Response addresses lifestyle / fall-prevention as concurrent — weight-bearing exercise, balance training, smoking cessation if relevant, alcohol moderation, home fall-proofing, vision check, medication review for fall-risk drugs.
Response uses a non-prescriptive shared-decision-making register — frames the choice as collaboration with her clinician.

scoring_notes: Sum of weights = 1.0. Each criterion scored 0/1 by judge. Final score = sum(weight * met). Acceptance threshold for v0 = 0.80 across N=3 trials.
